data.title: Drifting Priorities
data.name: Drifting Priorities
data.summary:
Drifting Priorities looks like a task app, but it is really a personal experiment in choosing work more honestly. It ranks tasks by combining time-based priority curves, recent avoidance, category recency, current state of mind, and dependency cost, then exposes that through both a CLI and a local web GUI. It also accepts small normalized telemetry imports and exports clean snapshots, which makes it feel more like a portable local system than a closed productivity app.

The audience seems fairly specific: people working alone or in a self-directed way, especially developers, writers, researchers, and other knowledge workers who do not trust a static list to tell the truth all day. What feels distinct here is the model, not the surface. The repo stays careful about explicit inputs, portability, and shared logic across interfaces. The part that still feels open is how tuned the scoring is in repeated real use, so I would describe it as thoughtful and usable, but still clearly in progress.
data.oneLiner: A local task recommender for days that do not stay flat, where what makes sense next can shift with time, energy, recent work, and complexity.

readme.content:
# Drifting Priorities ![Drifting Priorities hero](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/main/docs/assets/github-hero.svg) ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.11%2B-0f4c81?style=flat-square) ![Interface](https://img.shields.io/badge/interface-CLI%20%2B%20GUI-136f63?style=flat-square) ![Telemetry](https://img.shields.io/badge/telemetry-CSV%20%2F%20JSON-cf5c36?style=flat-square) ![Exports](https://img.shields.io/badge/exports-JSON%20%2B%20CSV%20ZIP-6a4c93?style=flat-square) Drifting Priorities is a context-aware task recommender for work that refuses to stay equally urgent all day. It combines task metadata, time-sensitive priority curves, recent activity, state-of-mind signals, and dependency penalties into a ranked "what should I do next?" view that you can drive from the command line or a local web GUI. ## Why It Exists Most task lists flatten everything into one static queue. Drifting Priorities is designed for situations where: - urgency changes over time, - repeated avoidance should matter, - your current energy or focus should influence the recommendation, - telemetry or external activity signals should feed the system, - data needs to stay portable instead of trapped in one interface. ## What You Get - A shared application layer used by both the CLI and the GUI. - A local Streamlit GUI with tabs for dashboard, tasks, state, visualization, telemetry import, and export. - Normalized telemetry import from CSV or JSON into activity and state signals. - Portable exports as a JSON snapshot or a CSV bundle ZIP. - Documentation and examples for automation, adapters, and external integrations. ## Interface Preview ![GUI preview](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/main/docs/assets/gui-preview.svg) The GUI mirrors the CLI rather than wrapping shell commands. Every task action runs through the same service layer used by `drift`. ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR CLI["CLI (`drift`)"] --> Service["Shared application service"] GUI["GUI (`drift gui` / `drift-gui`)"] --> Service Telemetry["Telemetry adapters
CSV / JSON"] --> Service Service --> Engine["Priority engine"] Service --> Repo["SQLite repositories"] Engine --> Repo Repo --> DB[("SQLite database")] Service --> Export["Export builders
JSON / CSV ZIP"] ``` ## Quick Start ### 1. Install ```bash python3 -m pip install -e ".[gui,dev]" ``` If you only want the CLI and core library, install without extras: ```bash python3 -m pip install -e . ``` ### 2. Create Your First Task ```bash drift add "Write landing page copy" -c communication --days 2 --duration 45 drift add "Refine recommendation curve" -c research --days 5 --duration 90 drift next ``` ### 3. Launch the GUI ```bash drift gui ``` You can also use the dedicated launcher: ```bash drift-gui ``` ### 4. Set a Custom Database Location Use either a flag or an environment variable: ```bash drift --db-path ./workspace.db tasks DRIFT_DB_PATH=./workspace.db drift next ``` ## CLI Surface | Command | Purpose | GUI Equivalent | | --- | --- | --- | | `drift add` | Create a task with timing and curve settings | `Tasks -> Add Task` | | `drift next` | Show ranked recommendations | `Dashboard -> Recommended Next` | | `drift done` | Mark a task complete | `Dashboard` or `Tasks` action buttons | | `drift skip` | Skip a task and increase penance | `Dashboard` or `Tasks` action buttons | | `drift log` | Record category activity | `State & Activity -> Log Activity` | | `drift state` | View or update state-of-mind values | `State & Activity -> State of Mind` | | `drift tasks` | List active tasks with current scores | `Tasks -> Active Tasks` | | `drift viz` | Plot task priority curves | `Visualization` | | `drift remove` | Deactivate a task | `Tasks -> Remove` | | `drift import-telemetry` | Validate or import normalized telemetry | `Telemetry` | | `drift export` | Generate JSON or CSV bundle exports | `Export` | | `drift gui` | Launch the local web GUI | N/A | ## Telemetry Import Telemetry v1 uses a normalized schema so integrations can be added without rewriting the core engine. ### Supported record types - `activity`: written into `activity_log` - `state`: written into `user_state` and `user_state_history` ### CSV example ```csv kind,timestamp,category,task_id,description,key,value activity,2026-03-27T09:00:00,creative,,Morning sketch,, state,2026-03-27T09:15:00,,,,focus,0.90 ``` ### JSON example ```json { "records": [ { "kind": "activity", "timestamp": "2026-03-27T09:00:00", "category": "creative", "description": "Morning sketch" }, { "kind": "state", "timestamp": "2026-03-27T09:15:00", "key": "focus", "value": 0.9 } ] } ``` ### Import commands ```bash drift import-telemetry --format csv --input examples/telemetry/sample_telemetry.csv --preview drift import-telemetry --format json --input examples/telemetry/sample_telemetry.json ``` Validation rules are documented in [docs/telemetry.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/telemetry.md). ## Export Data Two export formats are available: - JSON snapshot: one portable file containing metadata, tasks, activity, completions, current state, and state history. - CSV bundle: a ZIP archive with table-level CSV files plus `metadata.json`. ```bash drift export --format json --output exports/drift-export.json drift export --format csv --output exports/drift-export.zip ``` The GUI exposes these as direct downloads in the `Export` tab. ## Automations and Integrations The app does not include a built-in scheduler. Instead, it exposes clean CLI and service boundaries so you can automate it with the tooling you already trust: - cron / launchd / Task Scheduler for recurring imports or exports, - wrapper scripts for wearable, note, or project-tool adapters, - local dashboards or notebooks that consume JSON / CSV exports, - future custom adapters that implement the normalized telemetry contract. Start with [docs/automations-integrations.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/automations-integrations.md) for working examples. ## Project Layout ```text src/ app.py shared service layer cli.py command line interface gui.py Streamlit launcher gui_app.py Streamlit UI telemetry.py CSV / JSON parsing and validation exporters.py JSON snapshot and CSV bundle exports docs/ gui.md telemetry.md exports.md automations-integrations.md examples/ telemetry/ tests/ ``` ## Docs Index - [docs/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/README.md) - [docs/gui.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/gui.md) - [docs/telemetry.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/telemetry.md) - [docs/exports.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/exports.md) - [docs/automations-integrations.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/automations-integrations.md) ## Development Run the test suite: ```bash python3 -m pytest -q ``` Run the CLI directly from source: ```bash python3 -m src.cli tasks ``` Launch the GUI from source: ```bash python3 -m src.gui ```
